name: Ring Video Doorbell
short_description: A Wi-Fi-connected video doorbell that streams live video, detects motion, and sends alerts to a smartphone when someone approaches the door.
long_description: Introduced in 2014 (evolving from founder Jamie Siminoff's earlier Doorbot), the Ring Video Doorbell pairs a camera, microphone, speaker, and motion sensors with a phone app so a household can see and speak to visitors from anywhere. It turns the front door into an always-watching sensing node, and successive versions added computer-vision features such as person and package detection, motion zones, and AI-generated descriptions of who or what is at the door. As a mass-market device, it shows how ambient physical AI embeds perception and notification into everyday domestic infrastructure, while also fueling debates about home surveillance and privacy.

images.caption: A Ring video doorbell mounted on a stone surface beside a front door.
images.alt_text: A black-and-silver Ring video doorbell with a round camera lens and a lit blue ring around the button, mounted on a textured stone wall.

interactions.title: Know the moment a box hits the porch
interactions.description: Draw a package zone over the doormat and the doorbell pings your phone the instant a delivery is dropped there, sorting it apart from a passing person, a car, or a swaying branch.

interactions.title: A sentence instead of a thumbnail
interactions.description: Rather than a generic 'motion detected,' the app surfaces an AI-written line like 'a person is walking up the driveway carrying a box,' so you can decide whether to even open the clip.

interactions.title: Let the doorbell answer when you can't
interactions.description: Set Quick Replies and a visitor hears a pre-set message — 'Please leave the package by the door' — and can speak a reply back, turning the doorbell into an answering machine for the front step.

interactions.title: Yell through the door from the office
interactions.description: Homeowners watching the live feed from elsewhere use two-way talk to shout 'Get away from the door!' at people testing the handle — and the would-be intruders bolt, startled by a voice from an empty porch.

interactions.title: The bear that rang the bell
interactions.description: Motion meant for porch pirates instead catches wildlife: black bears repeatedly walk up, sniff the lens, and nudge the button with a nose or paw, sending the household a 'someone's at the door' alert at 1 a.m.

interactions.title: Police asking the neighborhood for footage
interactions.description: Through the Neighbors app, departments post Community Requests after a nearby crime and owners choose whether to hand over clips from a given window — quietly turning a doorbell into part of a distributed evidence network.

interactions.title: A standing porch log for the household
interactions.description: Because it already writes plain-language descriptions of each event, the doorbell could compile an end-of-day digest — 'two deliveries, the dog walker at 4, an unknown person who lingered at 9' — so you skim the day at the door instead of scrubbing clips.

interactions.title: Greet the regulars by routine, not by face
interactions.description: Using only its existing person, vehicle, and timing signals, the doorbell could learn the rhythm of expected arrivals — the mail truck mid-morning, the school bus at 3 — and stay quiet for those while flagging anything that breaks the pattern.
